---
title: "Day 2 (Tuesday): Trees and Modern Pipelines"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../assets/css/slides-theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    incremental: true
    scrollable: true
    slide-level: 2
---

## Learning objectives

- Contrast statistical and algorithmic modeling cultures
- Build intuition for decision trees
- Use `tidymodels` as the core pipeline grammar

## Navigation

- [Teaching Hub](../index.qmd)
- [Curriculum Outline](../curriculum-outline.qmd)
- [Module Library](../modules/index.qmd)
- [Back: Monday deck](day-01-monday.qmd)
- [Next: Thursday deck](day-03-thursday.qmd)

## Two cultures framing

- Mathematical culture: interpretable parametric assumptions
- Algorithmic culture: prediction-focused flexible learners
- In practice, good workflows combine both perspectives

## Why trees now?

- Trees model nonlinear effects and interactions naturally
- Easy to explain split logic to students
- Great bridge from parametric models to ensembles

## Decision tree mechanics

- Recursive partitioning of feature space
- Impurity criteria (Gini, entropy)
- Stopping rules and pruning to control complexity

## Demo: tree splitting intuition

- Optional: draw one 2D partition by hand or use a single `rpart` plot in the console; full coding practice belongs in exercises.

## Pipeline mindset

- Reproducibility and leakage prevention come first
- Preprocessing is part of the model, not a side step
- Evaluation design must be fixed before tuning

## Tidymodels: split objects first (`rsample`)

- `initial_split(data, prop = 0.8, strata = outcome)` returns an `rsplit` bundle: which rows belong to analysis vs assessment.
- `training(split)` and `testing(split)` materialize the two tibbles. **Never** preprocess the full dataset before splitting if steps learn from data (means, factor levels, PCA loadings).
- For repeated estimates of generalization error, use `vfold_cv(training(split), v = 10, strata = outcome)` or nested resampling when tuning within an outer loop.

## Recipes: deferred transformations (`recipes`)

- A **recipe** is a declarative list of steps: `recipe(outcome ~ predictors, data = train_data) |> step_*()`.
- **Roles:** `all_numeric_predictors()`, `all_nominal_predictors()`, `all_outcomes()` select columns by role instead of fragile name vectors.
- Common steps: `step_impute_*`, `step_normalize`, `step_dummy`, `step_zv` (zero-variance), `step_corr` (remove redundant columns), `step_log`, `step_ns` (splines).
- `prep(recipe, training = train_data)` learns constants from training rows; `bake(prep, new_data = ...)` applies the same rules to validation/test rows. This is what prevents leakage.

## Parsnip: specification vs estimation (`parsnip`)

- **Specification** describes the model family and hyperparameters, not the fitted object: e.g. `logistic_reg(penalty = tune(), mixture = 1) |> set_engine("glmnet") |> set_mode("classification")`.
- `set_engine()` picks the computational backend (`"glmnet"`, `"ranger"`, `"xgboost"`, `"keras"`). Same user-facing call can map to different packages.
- `translate()` shows how a parsnip call maps to the underlying function arguments—useful when debugging or reading tutorials written in native `xgboost` syntax.
- **Fit** happens later via `fit(workflow, data = train_data)` or `last_fit`, after workflows bundle preprocessing + model.

## Workflows: glue recipe + model (`workflows`)

- `workflow() |> add_recipe(rec) |> add_model(mod)` produces a single object you can resample and tune as a unit.
- Benefits: the resampler sees the *whole* estimation chain; tuning grids can include recipe parameters (e.g. spline degrees) alongside model hyperparameters.
- `workflow() |> add_formula(y ~ .) |> add_model(mod)` skips a recipe when you only need a bare formula, but recipes remain the right place for learned steps.

## Tuning and metrics (`tune`, `dials`, `yardstick`)

- Mark unknown parameters with `tune()` inside `parsnip` specs or `recipes` steps.
- `extract_parameter_set_dials(wf)` + `grid_regular()` / `grid_space_filling()` build candidate grids; `tune_grid(wf, resamples = folds, grid = ..., metrics = metric_set(roc_auc, pr_auc))` evaluates them.
- `collect_metrics()`, `autoplot()`, and `select_best()` summarize results; `finalize_workflow(wf, best_params)` plugs in winners.
- `yardstick::metric_set` keeps evaluation consistent across models; choose metrics **before** peeking at test data.

## Tidymodels blueprint (overview)

- `recipes`: preprocess, impute, encode, normalize
- `parsnip`: model specification
- `workflows`: combine recipe + model
- `rsample` + `tune`: resampling and hyperparameter search

## Mental checklist before you `fit`

1. Declare split and resampling strategy.
2. Build recipe on **training** data only; verify steps make sense for production features.
3. Choose parsnip model + engine + mode; wrap in workflow.
4. Pick metrics aligned with deployment costs; tune if needed.
5. Fit final workflow on all training data; assess once on the locked-away test set (or use nested CV for honest tuning comparison).

## Reusable classification template

- One recipe skeleton
- One resampling object
- Plug-in model specs (tree, forest, boosting, neural net)
- Comparable metrics and tuning framework

## Shared pipeline template (single source)

The **next slides** are included from `_includes/tidymodels-pipeline.qmd` on **Palmer Penguins** (Adelie vs Gentoo, same idea as [penguins-classification.Rmd](../notebooks/penguins-classification.Rmd)); for **sex** or **three-species** tasks, see the other penguin notebooks from the **Data** page.

{{< include ../_includes/tidymodels-pipeline.qmd >}}

## From `caret` to `tidymodels` (migration mental map)

- `caret::train(form, data, method, trControl, tuneGrid)` historically bundled preprocessing options; today **`tidymodels` separates concerns** more strictly (recipe vs model vs resampling).
- Mapping cheat-sheet: `trainControl` \(\rightarrow\) `control_*()` functions; `method` string \(\rightarrow\) `parsnip` + `set_engine()`; `preProcess` \(\rightarrow\) `recipe` steps; `varImp` \(\rightarrow\) `vip` or model-specific tools.
- Why switch: composability, safer tuning, easier alignment with **feature-store** style pipelines and production bundles (`vetiver`, Posit Connect).

## Ensembles at the API level (`stacks`, `workflowsets`)

- **`workflowsets`** runs many `(recipe, model)` pairs across the same resamples—ideal for homework comparing five learners fairly.
- **`stacks`** treats tuned workflows as level-0 candidates and learns a sparse combination (often a constrained glm) as a meta-learner—nice capstone after random forests and boosting.
- Pedagogically: show **one** `workflowset` call on Thursday after students understand single workflows.

## Transition to Thursday

- Tuesday gives the pipeline backbone
- Thursday swaps in stronger learners and richer evaluation
- Exercises are handled separately
